data.title: Jesco Holdings (TSE:1434) Margin Improvement To 7.2% Tests Earnings Volatility Concerns
data.description:
Jesco Holdings (TSE:1434) has put up another solid quarter, with Q2 2026 revenue of ¥5,829 million and basic EPS of ¥63.08, supported by net income of ¥438 million as the latest set of results lands on investors’ desks. The company has seen revenue move from ¥3,876 million and EPS of ¥39.72 in Q2 2025 to ¥5,829 million and EPS of ¥63.08 in Q2 2026. Trailing twelve month EPS of ¥219.92 and net income of ¥1,526 million provide additional context around the earnings run rate and margin profile. For investors, this earnings print puts the focus squarely on how sustainably Jesco is converting sales into profits and what that means for margins over the next stretch.

See our full analysis for Jesco Holdings.

With the headline numbers in place, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing narratives around Jesco Holdings to see which stories the data supports and which views might need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

75% earnings growth in the last year

  • Trailing 12 month net income sits at ¥1,526 million with basic EPS of ¥219.92, compared with the stated 75% earnings growth rate over the last year and a five year earnings growth pace of 20.1% per year.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that this 75% earnings growth lines up with higher trailing net income and EPS, which heavily supports the idea of stronger business performance than the longer term 20.1% annual average. However, the pattern of quarterly EPS ranging from a loss in Q3 2025 to ¥104.80 in Q4 2025 and then ¥56.65 and ¥63.08 in Q1 and Q2 2026 is a reminder that results can swing around within that growth story.
    • Supporters pointing to improved profitability can reference the move in trailing net profit margin from 4.9% to 7.2%, alongside the higher trailing EPS of ¥219.92.
    • At the same time, the Q3 2025 loss of ¥32 million and negative EPS of ¥4.61 show that even within a strong year, individual periods have carried project or timing effects that are less friendly to the bullish case.

Margins at 7.2% with a steadier recent run

  • The trailing net profit margin of 7.2%, up from 4.9% the previous year, sits alongside recent quarterly net income of ¥393 million in Q1 2026 and ¥438 million in Q2 2026, with revenue of ¥5,105 million and ¥5,829 million. This shows profits and sales moving together over the last two reported quarters.
  • Supportive for a bullish angle, the 7.2% margin and positive net income across the last four quarters to Q2 2026 align with the higher trailing EPS, yet the earlier Q3 2025 loss of ¥32 million and negative EPS of ¥4.61 keep the focus on how consistently that 7.2% margin can be repeated.
    • Backers of the stronger profitability story can highlight that trailing revenue reached ¥21,314 million against net income of ¥1,526 million, which is consistent with the reported margin step up.
    • On the other hand, the quarterly spread from ¥3,637 million of revenue in Q3 2025 to ¥6,743 million in Q4 2025 shows that volume and mix can move around, which is a useful check on very aggressive bullish expectations around margin stability.

Curious how these margin trends and earnings swings are shaping the bigger picture investors are talking about right now? See what the community is saying about Jesco Holdings.

P/E of 10.4x and price above DCF fair value

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 10.4x, which is below the broader JP market at 14.7x and the JP Construction industry at 12.6x, yet above the peer average of 9.6x. The current share price of ¥2,289 sits above the stated DCF fair value of ¥1,607.03 and alongside a dividend yield of 2.1% and higher share price volatility over the past three months than the JP market.
  • Critics who are cautious on valuation can point to the current price being higher than the DCF fair value and the richer P/E than peers, which matches the idea that recent profit strength and a 2.1% yield are already reflected in the price to some extent, especially when set against the higher recent volatility.
    • Bears highlighting valuation risk can reference the gap between the ¥2,289 share price and the ¥1,607.03 DCF fair value, alongside the peer P/E of 9.6x that sits below Jesco’s 10.4x.
    • At the same time, investors who compare across the market can see that the P/E is still under the JP market at 14.7x and below the 12.6x industry average, so the caution is more about where Jesco sits within its peer group and relative to the DCF number than about an extreme market wide premium.

data.title: Is Highlander Silver’s (TSX:HSLV) Staged Corani Plan Redefining Its Capital and Risk Playbook?

  • A distinctive aspect of the update is the company’s plan to deliver an updated feasibility study using a staged-development approach, supported by experienced Peruvian and international consultants and contractors to help manage capital, funding, timeline and execution risks.
  • Next, we’ll examine how the staged-development feasibility study at Corani could shape Highlander Silver’s investment narrative in the months ahead.

To own Highlander Silver today, you have to believe that folding Corani into the story can justify a high price-to-book multiple and years of losses by eventually turning a zero‑revenue explorer into a producing silver company. The latest update is material for near-term catalysts: it moves Corani from concept to visible execution, with airborne surveys, an active drill program and concrete site works feeding into a staged-development feasibility study due by the end of Q3 2026. That concentrates attention on two things in the months ahead: the quality of exploration and technical results, and how credibly management can frame capital needs after a year of heavy equity issuance. At the same time, the same news also sharpens the biggest risk right now: a capital‑intensive project run by a relatively new board and management team, with no operating cash flow to fall back on if conditions tighten.

data.title: Catering International Services (ENXTPA:ALCIS) Margin Improvement Reinforces Bullish Earnings Momentum Narrative
data.description:
Catering International & Services Société Anonyme (ENXTPA:ALCIS) has just posted its FY 2025 numbers with first half revenue of €236.5 million, basic EPS of €0.52 and net income of €4.0 million, set against trailing 12 month revenue of €489.2 million and net income of €9.1 million. The company has seen revenue move from €199.5 million and EPS of €0.21 in 1H 2024 to €223.4 million and EPS of €0.41 in 2H 2024, reaching €236.5 million and EPS of €0.52 in 1H 2025, a path that lines up with an 86.9% trailing earnings uplift and margins that are starting to look more supportive for holders focused on profit quality.

See our full analysis for Catering International & Services Société Anonyme.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile matches or challenges the most widely shared narratives around Catering International & Services Société Anonyme.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM earnings 86.9% higher than a year ago

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, net income is €9.1 million, up from €4.9 million a year earlier, which lines up with the 86.9% earnings growth figure cited in the analysis data.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is how this trailing earnings jump sits alongside steadily higher half year profits, with net income reported at €1.6 million in 1H 2024, €3.2 million in 2H 2024 and €4.0 million in 1H 2025, which supports the idea that recent performance is being built over several reporting periods rather than coming from a single outlier half.
    • Supporters who focus on earnings momentum can point to this run of €1.6 million, €3.2 million and €4.0 million as concrete evidence behind the 18.1% yearly earnings growth forecast referenced in the risk and reward data.
    • At the same time, anyone testing the bullish case can keep in mind that this pattern is based on the specific 18 month window shown, so future periods will be important to see whether this rhythm in net income continues.

To see how this pattern of rising profits fits into longer term growth expectations and valuation checks, it helps to look at a broader narrative built around the same numbers, not just this reporting period. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Catering International & Services Société Anonyme.

Margins edge up to 1.9% TTM

  • The trailing 12 month net margin sits at 1.9% compared with 1.2% a year earlier, which is consistent with net income of €9.1 million on €489.2 million of revenue versus €4.9 million on €422.8 million previously.
  • Supporters of a bullish narrative often point to this kind of margin shift as evidence that operations are becoming more efficient, and the figures here offer some backing for that view while still leaving room for debate.
    • On one hand, the move from roughly €4.9 million to €9.1 million of trailing profit while revenue moves from €422.8 million to €489.2 million gives bulls a clear link between higher sales and fatter profits in the recent period.
    • On the other hand, margins at 1.9% are still fairly slim in absolute terms, so anyone leaning on the bullish case needs to stay aware that profitability is sensitive to even small shifts in costs or pricing.

P/E of 12.9x and DCF fair value gap

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 12.9x, below both the peer average of 21.4x and the wider European Commercial Services average of 16.3x, while the supplied DCF fair value of €36.17 sits well above the current €15.10 share price.
  • What is interesting for bullish investors is how this combination of a lower P/E and a DCF fair value level that is much higher than the current price lines up with the strong trailing earnings growth, because it suggests that the valuation multiples have not moved in step with the 86.9% year on year earnings gain.
    • Supporters of the bullish angle may argue that if earnings continue to track the 18.1% yearly earnings growth forecast, a P/E of 12.9x and a DCF estimate of €36.17 both point to a valuation that does not fully reflect the recent profit run.
    • More cautious investors can still use the same figures to stress test the bullish stance by asking whether the improvement in net margin to 1.9% on €489.2 million of revenue is enough to justify closing the gap between €15.10 and the DCF fair value.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of CN¥230.24 per share. Against the current Hong Kong share price of HK$107.30, this implies that, on this method, the stock is 53.4% undervalued.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase (JPM) Net Margin Slip Tests Bullish Premium Ahead Of Q1 2026 Earnings
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) has kicked off 2026 with investors weighing a recent stretch of strong top line and solid profitability, capped by Q4 2025 revenue of US$41.1b and basic EPS of US$4.64 on net income of US$12.7b. Over the past year, the bank has seen revenue move from US$40.1b in Q4 2024 to US$41.1b in Q4 2025, while trailing twelve month basic EPS sat at US$20.05 on net income of US$55.7b. This sets the stage for a results season where the focus is firmly on how comfortably margins are being held.

Margins Hold, But TTM Net Margin Eases To 33.1%

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, JPMorgan’s net income of US$55.7b on revenue of US$168.2b implies a net margin of 33.1%, slightly lower than the 34.1% margin cited for the prior year.
  • Consensus narrative highlights technology, payments, and wealth growth as supports for long term margins. However, the 1 percentage point margin slip and a cost to income ratio holding at 52% show that higher expenses and regulatory and tech spending are already affecting profitability even as fee and digital revenue stay solid.

Valuation Premium Versus Banks, With DCF Gap

  • JPMorgan trades on a trailing P/E of 15x compared with 13.7x for peers and 11.9x for the wider US Banks industry, while the current share price of US$311.12 sits below a cited DCF fair value of about US$427.22.
  • Bulls argue that high quality earnings and five year EPS growth of 8.1% a year justify a richer multiple. At the same time, the mix of a 33.1% net margin, forecast earnings growth of about 3.94% a year, and a 1.93% dividend yield means investors weighing the bullish case need to decide whether the premium P/E and the DCF gap together leave enough room for the upside they are targeting.
    • Supporters of the bullish narrative point to JPMorgan’s push in digital banking, global payments, and wealth management as reasons that long term earnings growth could track closer to optimistic scenarios than to the more modest growth figures in the base forecasts.
    • In addition, the requirement in the bullish narrative for a higher future P/E than today, on top of an already above industry multiple, depends heavily on that growth and margin story holding up against competitive and regulatory pressure.

Bulls who think the current numbers only tell half the story may want to see how the optimistic case connects tech, payments, and wealth growth into a longer term view of earnings durability and valuation 🐂 JPMorgan Chase Bull Case

Credit Quality And Expenses Feed Bear Concerns

  • Total loans increased from US$1.35t in Q4 2024 to US$1.49t in Q4 2025, while non performing loans moved from US$8.8b to US$9.9b over the same period and the cost to income ratio held at 52% on a trailing twelve month view.
  • Bears worry that rising credit costs and higher expenses could pressure future profits, and the data gives them some support with non performing loans ticking up across the last few quarters and forecasts calling for revenue growth of about 4.1% a year and earnings growth of roughly 3.94% a year, both below the broader US market forecasts.

    • On top of that, the cost to income ratio at 52% on a trailing basis leaves less room for profit if credit costs or compliance spending continue to absorb more of each revenue dollar, which is a key concern in the bearish narrative.

data.title: Companies Like Hazer Group (ASX:HZR) Can Afford To Invest In Growth
data.description:
There's no doubt that money can be made by owning shares of unprofitable businesses. For example, although software-as-a-service business Salesforce.com lost money for years while it grew recurring revenue, if you held shares since 2005, you'd have done very well indeed. But the harsh reality is that very many loss making companies burn through all their cash and go bankrupt.

So, the natural question for Hazer Group (ASX:HZR) shareholders is whether they should be concerned by its rate of cash burn. For the purposes of this article, cash burn is the annual rate at which an unprofitable company spends cash to fund its growth; its negative free cash flow. The first step is to compare its cash burn with its cash reserves, to give us its 'cash runway'.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Does Hazer Group Have A Long Cash Runway?

A cash runway is defined as the length of time it would take a company to run out of money if it kept spending at its current rate of cash burn. When Hazer Group last reported its December 2025 balance sheet in February 2026, it had zero debt and cash worth AU$15m. Looking at the last year, the company burnt through AU$4.3m. Therefore, from December 2025 it had 3.5 years of cash runway. A runway of this length affords the company the time and space it needs to develop the business. The image below shows how its cash balance has been changing over the last few years.

See our latest analysis for Hazer Group

How Is Hazer Group's Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

In the last year, Hazer Group did book revenue of AU$7.3m, but its revenue from operations was less, at just AU$87k. We don't think that's enough operating revenue for us to understand too much from revenue growth rates, since the company is growing off a low base. So we'll focus on the cash burn, today. Notably, its cash burn was actually down by 71% in the last year, which is a real positive in terms of resilience, but uninspiring when it comes to investment for growth. Clearly, however, the crucial factor is whether the company will grow its business going forward. For that reason, it makes a lot of sense to take a look at our analyst forecasts for the company.

How Hard Would It Be For Hazer Group To Raise More Cash For Growth?

There's no doubt Hazer Group's rapidly reducing cash burn brings comfort, but even if it's only hypothetical, it's always worth asking how easily it could raise more money to fund further growth. Issuing new shares, or taking on debt, are the most common ways for a listed company to raise more money for its business. Commonly, a business will sell new shares in itself to raise cash and drive growth. By looking at a company's cash burn relative to its market capitalisation, we gain insight on how much shareholders would be diluted if the company needed to raise enough cash to cover another year's cash burn.

Since it has a market capitalisation of AU$104m, Hazer Group's AU$4.3m in cash burn equates to about 4.1% of its market value. That's a low proportion, so we figure the company would be able to raise more cash to fund growth, with a little dilution, or even to simply borrow some money.

Is Hazer Group's Cash Burn A Worry?

It may already be apparent to you that we're relatively comfortable with the way Hazer Group is burning through its cash. In particular, we think its cash runway stands out as evidence that the company is well on top of its spending. But it's fair to say that its cash burn relative to its market cap was also very reassuring. Taking all the factors in this report into account, we're not at all worried about its cash burn, as the business appears well capitalized to spend as needs be. Another way we can view these estimates is in the context of the bigger picture, such as how the forecasts stack up against past performance, and whether forecasts are more or less bullish relative to other companies in the industry. The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. Fortunately, they also reconfirmed their revenue numbers, suggesting that it's tracking in line with expectations. Additionally, our data suggests that revenue is expected to grow faster than the wider industry. There was no real change to the consensus price target, suggesting that the intrinsic value of the business has not undergone any major changes with the latest estimates.

This MEG Energy deal is mainly about scale and control. By combining into a producer targeting more than 720,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, Cenovus gains a larger, more contiguous oil sands footprint and potentially more influence over project sequencing and operating choices. Integration with Cenovus refineries and transportation assets could create room to optimize blends, capture more value between the mine and the refinery gate, and smooth out some Western Canadian Select pricing pressure. For you, the key question is whether management can translate that larger asset base into lower unit costs and steadier cash generation, without stretching the balance sheet or diluting returns on capital.

How This Fits Into The Cenovus Energy Narrative

  • The acquisition aligns with the existing focus on long life oil sands projects and operational efficiency. It may reinforce the idea of higher, more stable production over time.
  • It could also test the narrative around capital discipline, as integrating MEG adds complexity, execution risk, and ongoing investment needs that might compete with shareholder returns.
  • The current narrative centers on existing projects like Narrows Lake and West White Rose. This transaction introduces portfolio and integration factors that are not fully captured in those project specific discussions.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Integration risk from combining two large oil sands platforms, with potential for higher than expected costs or delays.
  • ⚠️ Increased exposure to regulatory and ESG scrutiny on Canadian oil sands, which could affect long term project economics.
  • 🎁 Greater scale in Synthetic Heavy Oil Sands, which may support operating efficiencies and more options for capital allocation.
  • 🎁 A larger integrated footprint that could give Cenovus more flexibility in managing crude quality, transportation, and refinery utilization compared with peers such as Suncor Energy and Canadian Natural Resources.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how Cenovus structures the combined balance sheet, the integration timeline, and any updated production or cost guidance once MEG is included. Watch for comments on capital spending priorities, especially how management balances growth projects, debt reduction, and shareholder returns. It is also worth tracking any shifts in regulatory signals or carbon policy that could influence long life oil sands assets. For context, keep an eye on how other Canadian integrated producers handle scale, emissions, and capital discipline, as that will help you judge whether Cenovus is using this acquisition to strengthen its competitive position or simply increase its size.
